toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ## Developer Complaint
2. 
3. Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
4. for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
5. while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
6. 
7. We've already verified:
8. - No analyzers are slowing things down
9. - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
10. - The project itself compiles in <500ms
11. 
12. The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
13. project graph on every CLI invocation.
14. 
15. We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
16. command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.
17.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

data.content:
Here's what's happening and how to fix it:

## Root Cause

Every `dotnet build` invocation spawns a new MSBuild process, re-parses the project file, and re-evaluates the SDK imports — even when nothing changed. Visual Studio avoids this by keeping MSBuild resident in-process.

## Recommendations

**1. Enable the MSBuild Server (biggest win)** — .NET 8+ supports a long-lived MSBuild server that caches project evaluation across invocations, just like VS:

```sh
export DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1
```

Or set it permanently in a `Directory.Build.props`:

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <!--
      Keep a long-lived MSBuild server process between CLI builds so that
      project evaluation and SDK resolution are cached across invocations.
      This eliminates the 2-4 s startup overhead reported on incremental builds.
    -->
    <MSBuildServer Condition="'$(BuildingInsideVisualStudio)' != 'true'">true</MSBuildServer>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
The developer report explains the issue clearly: CLI builds with `dotnet build` are slow for incremental builds because of MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the project graph on every CLI invocation. Visual Studio doesn't have this problem because it keeps MSBuild loaded in-process.

The key solution here is to use the **MSBuild Server** (also known as the MSBuild build server or `dotnet build-server`). Starting with .NET 8, MSBuild has a long-lived server process that can be reused across builds, avoiding the startup and project evaluation overhead.

The main recommendations:

1. **Enable the MSBuild Server** — By setting `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` environment variable or adding `<MSBuildServer>true</MSBuildServer>` to the project. This keeps MSBuild running between builds, eliminating startup overhead. (This is available in .NET 8+)


2. **Use `dotnet watch`** — For iterative development, this keeps the process alive and only rebuilds what changed.
3. **Leverage node reuse** — The MSBuild server approach reuses nodes across invocations, which is the core mechanism that eliminates the overhead from repeated startup and project graph re-evaluation.

The real win here is the MSBuild server enabled by `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` in .NET 8+. It maintains a long-lived process that caches project evaluation, so subsequent builds skip the expensive initialization phase—exactly what Visual Studio does internally. I should also make sure node reuse isn't disabled and consider using static graph evaluation for multi-project solutions. The best approach is to set these optimizations at the repo level through a `Directory.Build.props` file.

    -->

</Project>

data.content:
**2. Ensure MSBuild node reuse isn't disabled** — Check that `MSBUILDDISABLENODEREUSE=1` is **not** set in your environment. Node reuse lets MSBuild worker processes persist between builds.

**3. Use `dotnet watch` for the inner loop** — It keeps the build host alive and rebuilds only on file changes:
```sh
dotnet watch build
```

**4. Use `dotnet run --no-build`** — After a build, run without re-triggering evaluation if you haven't changed code.

## Summary

| Technique | Saves | How |
|---|---|---|
| **MSBuild Server** | ~1.5–3 s startup/eval | `Directory.Build.props` (created above) |
| **Node reuse** | ~0.5–1 s node spawn | Don't set `MSBUILDDISABLENODEREUSE=1` |
| **`dotnet watch`** | All re-invocation cost | Persistent host process |

The `Directory.Build.props` I created enables the MSBuild server for CLI builds only (skipped inside VS where it's unnecessary). This alone should bring incremental CLI builds close to the VS experience.
